confirmed.title: Tier-2/gate-2 'rule passed' verdicts fail pre-registered rule 1's invalid-transition clause: 13 spurious edit-units over 72 invalid transitions vs a copy floor of exactly 0, passed only via a post-hoc median-only reading
confirmed.location: lines 66, 68 (findings 16-17); rule text terminal-jepa/models/tier2.py:25-31
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 16 'slotpred-v2 passes the rule' and finding 17 'Gate 2 PASSED ... rule passed'
confirmed.description: Rule 1 (pre-registered, models/tier2.py:26-28) requires h1 rollout error on valid-no-op AND invalid transitions to match the copy floor (0) within noise, with AUC >= 0.9, and pre-specifies no summary statistic. slotpred-v2's invalid h1 error is mean 0.18055... = exactly 13 edit-units over 72 invalid transitions (rng replay of random.Random("dynamics:0") confirms type counts 353/75/72) on draws pair-matched with the copy control, which scores exactly 0/72 with zero variance (invalid => state unchanged => copy exact) — not "within noise" of the floor under any defensible statistical reading (paired sign test p≈2.4e-4; the ~18% over-fire rate is independently corroborated by train_log.json em.invalid 0.8141 at step 3000). The recorded PASS relies on scoring the clause by the median (0.0) — a statistic that saturates at 0 for any over-fire rate below 50% and is blind to exactly the spurious-edit failure the clause was written to catch — and the doc's pass sentences in findings 16 and 17 recite only the no-op error and AUC, omitting the invalid clause and demoting the mean to a "flagged caveat"/"inherited caveat". The round-1→2 loss fix introduced the over-fire (round-1 slotpred invalid mean 0.0/72) and median scoring could not detect the regression. Under the strict reading no round-2 candidate is calibrated (editpred-v2 fails AUC 0.785 and invalid under any statistic), so rule 2's "among calibrated candidates" set is empty. Gate 2 inherits invalid = 0.18055... verbatim in both codebook batteries (clean and dirty) and is likewise declared "rule passed". This matters at gate 3: finding 18 shows the validity filter is worth +46-53 points, i.e. unfiltered planners lean on exactly the invalid-transition class where this model over-fires — the plan's pre-recorded §5 "planner exploits undertrained invalid actions" risk. CORRECTION to the original finding: the claim that the doc applied a "different (harsher) metric" to fail editpred is wrong — editpred's calibration clause was also quoted by median (round-1 no-op median 1.0; editpred-v2 invalid change-magnitude median 1.0) and editpred fails under every statistic including AUC; the statistic use is consistent, the defect is that the median-only reading was post-hoc (never pre-registered) and insensitive to this failure mode. What survives: the bake-off winner (slotpred dominates editpred on every component in both rounds), the no-op clause, and the AUC clause as measured. What does not survive as recorded: finding 16's "passes the pre-registered rule" and finding 17's "rule passed".
confirmed.evidence: models/tier2.py:25-31 rule text; runs/tier2/slotpred-v2/battery.json rollout.h1_err_mean_by_type.invalid = 0.18055555555555555 (median 0), rng replay of the battery stream (random.Random('dynamics:0')) confirms type counts {state-changing: 353, valid-no-op: 75, invalid: 72}, 0.18055*72 = 13.0 exactly; runs/dynamics-battery/oracle-copy.json invalid mean/median 0.0 on the identical paired draws; runs/gate2/codebook-battery-{clean,dirty}.json inherit 0.18055...; runs/tier2/slotpred/battery.json round-1 invalid mean 0.0; runs/tier2/slotpred-v2/train_log.json em.invalid 0.8141 at step 3000; independent 720-transition CPU check: 23/113 invalid transitions fired; status doc findings 16/17 pass sentences quote only no-op error and AUC.

confirmed.title: Gate-1 'PASSED at ceiling' was never evaluated against its own pre-registered rule, and the architecture-matched untrained floor sits at the same path-keyed ceiling — the measurable margins fail both clauses
confirmed.location: lines 19, 26, 31, 62 (gate-1 rule, A1 pass declaration, finding 14); probes/frozen_probe.py:344-355
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 14 / 'gate 1 PASSED at ceiling' (held-out macro 100% path-keyed / 44.4% pooled vs the 28.2% untrained floor); repeated at lines 7, 19, 31
confirmed.description: The gate-1 rule was genuinely pre-registered (committed 2026-07-10, verified via git show HEAD): held-out linear macro >= architecture-matched untrained floor +20 AND >= reconstruction twin +10, with the §7 amendment pinning baselines to 'same encoder class and output dimension'. The recorded PASS uses neither comparison. (a) The floor cited, 28.2%, is the 256-d from-scratch untrained-CLS pooled floor — wrong architecture, wrong dimension, and wrong readout for the 768-d path-keyed headline. (b) A measured architecture-matched floor (random-init ModernBERT-base via AutoModel.from_config, identical readout and probe budget) gives content-given-existence 100.0% (2782/2782 held-out pairs) — margin exactly 0 on the headline metric — structural existence 1.0 by construction for ANY encoder (the keying is the parser), and cwd 42% at a data-starved fit, so the floor macro is >= 80.7% and the gate margin <= 19.3 < +20; two of the three macro components carry zero margin and the pass rides entirely on the never-measured cwd floor. (c) The reconstruction-twin +10 clause was never evaluated — the recon arm has never been run (status line 155) — and is unsatisfiable in principle at a 100% score, since the plan itself notes the twin sits at the probing ceiling on machine-readable observations. (d) The only readout that IS protocol-v2-comparable to the 28.2% floor, pooled, scores 44.4% — margin +16.2, also below +20. No predicate-satisfaction/exact-state metrics were reported as the rule requires. The substantive pooled result (frozen beats trained-CLS 26.0 and untrained-CLS 28.2) stands, but the gate-1 PASS as a rule verdict — the anchor of the whole A1 track — rests on margin arithmetic that was never done and fails where it can be done.
confirmed.evidence: git show HEAD:terminal-jepa-status.md confirms rule text (line 26) predates the run; runs/frozen-modernbert-v1/probe.json: pooled.linear.macro=0.44405, path_keyed macro 1.0, no untrained-frozen arm, no twin, no predicate-satisfaction keys; runs/untrained-cls-v1-v2.json: linear.macro=0.28171, encoder_d_out 256; margin 0.44405-0.28171=+16.2<20. Random-init ModernBERT check (script at scratchpad/random_floor_check.py: 300 fit / 200 eval states from data/v1, regime both, protocol-v2 fit_head/standardize, seed 0): cls_acc_given_exists=1.0, cwd_acc=0.42, macro_with_structural_exists=0.807; independently replicated at smaller budget (n=376 eval pairs) with content=1.000. Recon twin absence: status line 155; twin-at-ceiling note: terminal-jepa.md line 44.

confirmed.title: Gate-2 PASS validates no dynamics over frozen features: it is the unchanged tier-2 checkpoint re-scored behind a ~100% decoder that approximates the repo's own exact parser — bit-identical battery numbers, zero new capability evidence
confirmed.location: Gate2CodebookAdapter, lines 324-379; runs/gate2/codebook-battery-clean.json vs runs/tier2/slotpred-v2/battery.json; status doc line 68 (finding 17), line 31
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 17 'Gate 2 PASSED' as a distinct ladder rung, and the line-31 claim that the A1 arm cleared its dynamics gate ('the arm then trains a predictor on the frozen features and must clear gates 2-3 like everything else')
confirmed.description: The ladder defines gate 2 for A1 as a learned predictor over the frozen representation. That experiment ran twice and failed both times (rounds 1-2). The recorded PASS (round 3) swaps the interface: frozen features -> label-fit linear decoder -> the tier-2 predictor trained on ORACLE symbolic states (finding 16), reused unchanged — predict() and distance() delegate entirely to the tier-2 checkpoint. The frozen encoder's only role is encode(): text -> FsState, where existence is hand string-keying (no model involved), content class is a linear head over a line that literally prints the answer (render.py renders '{path} [cK]'; render_full docstring: 'Content classes are shown so the state is exactly recoverable'), and cwd over a line printing 'cwd: ...'. env/parse.py parse_full is the exact, round-trip-tested inverse and the plan's own nonprivileged component; plan §5 states 'in full-obs a trivial parser + classical search is a nonprivileged ceiling'. So the 149M encoder contributes a 99.7-100%-accurate approximation of a 25-line exact parser already in the repo, and the result is arithmetically forced by verdicts already on the books (findings 14 + 16): with decode at ~100% the composed system IS the tier-2 system, and the artifacts show it — state-changing h1 mean 0.29745042492917845 (=105/353), invalid 0.18055..., AUC 0.9075986868444231, and VoE 0.9683333... identical to 15+ digits between codebook-battery-clean.json and tier-2's battery.json (same battery rng, same 500 draws, decoder exact on every one); only goal-ranking moves by ~1.5/615 comparisons. The only new measurement in round 3 is the decoder's val accuracy, fit with ground-truth labels at build time and validated on the same 200 val trajectories the battery uses. The doc discloses the composition honestly, but the ladder bookkeeping treats a relabeling as a cleared gate; the gate the ladder actually specified for this arm remains failed, and gate 3 on this pipeline will measure 'parser + symbolic dynamics + planner' — the exact regime the plan says supports only machinery claims, not representation/dynamics claims.

confirmed.title: 'Closing A3 as unnecessary (measured, not argued)' is tautological: the predictor never sees contaminated inputs, so the pre-registered A3 fallback trigger was never exercised
confirmed.location: line 68 (finding 17, A3-closure sentence) and line 33 (A3 scope-down)
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 17's claim that the clean-vs-dirty battery identity is an end-to-end nuisance-robustness measurement that closes A3
confirmed.description: Finding 17's A3-closure sentence overclaims. In the passing round-3 pipeline the tier-2 predictor consumes only argmax-decoded discrete FsStates (models/gate2.py, Gate2CodebookAdapter.encode/predict), so the pre-registered fallback trigger (status line 33: "prediction over contaminated *inputs* still chases nuisance") was structurally unexercisable in that configuration — nuisance can only enter via a decode flip, and the decode's nuisance robustness (file_class 1.0 / cwd 0.9972, both regimes pooled) was already measured at codebook build time, making the clean/dirty battery identity near-foreordained: the dirty run's entire incremental content is one extra decode-error unit (state-changing h1 mean 106/353 vs 105/353; every other value bit-identical). One correction to the reviewer's wording: the trigger WAS exercised in feature space — rounds 1-2 ran clean/dirty battery pairs on the embedding-space arms (runs/gate2/battery-{clean,dirty}.log, battery-v2-{clean,dirty}.log) and showed large clean-vs-dirty gaps (e.g. round-1 calibration no-op change magnitude ~52.7 dirty vs ~1.27 clean; rollout h1 93 vs 58), unreported in the status doc — direct evidence that for arms where features rather than symbols flow into dynamics (A2, any future embedding-space predictor), input-side nuisance remains a live problem, consistent with finding 14's contaminated-feature measurements (pooled banner-swap ratio 1.17; banner decodable at 45%/31%). The closure therefore holds only conditional on keeping the symbolic bottleneck, which the sentence does not state.

confirmed.title: Filtered arms: position-0 pool replacement severs CEM's control of the only executed action, making cem-filtered ~ random-filtered by construction
confirmed.location: lines 97-113 (cem_plan sampling/refit), 101-102, 158 (receding horizon executes seq[0])
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 18's filtered pairing (73% vs 76%, delta -3) and its explanation 'the validity filter's +46-53 points is mostly needle-supply, which is why filtered random matches filtered CEM'
confirmed.description: In filtered mode every candidate's seq[0] is overwritten by an independent uniform draw from valid_pool AFTER the factored ids are sampled (line 102), but the elites refit (lines 110-113) uses the ORIGINAL ids at t=0 — ids that were discarded and had no causal relation to the rollout cost. So pv[0]/p1[0]/p2[0] are refit on noise and the distribution over position 0 stays uniform-over-pool in every iteration. Under receding horizon only seq[0] is ever executed, so filtered CEM has no first-order mechanism to sharpen the executed action; its only edge over filtered random shooting is second-order (sharpened suffixes changing which pool action wins argmin). The recorded -3 filtered delta is therefore close to guaranteed-null by harness construction, and the needle-supply explanation for 'filtered random matches filtered CEM' is confounded: the harness could not have shown a filtered CEM advantage even if one existed. This also deviates from plan §5, which specifies a filter that 'drops precondition-violating actions' (rejection, which would preserve distributional control of position 0), not replacement. The same harness is slated to run tiers 2-3 / gate 3 (§7's filtered-vs-filtered 'deployment condition'), so future filtered verdicts inherit the defect. The §7-primary unfiltered pairing is unaffected.
confirmed.evidence: Code reading of plan/cem.py: ids sampled at lines 98-99, seq[0] overwritten at line 102 (after ids fixed), cands store the stale ids (line 103), refit consumes e[2][t] for all t including t=0 (lines 110-113); cem_plan reinitializes all distributions uniform at every replan (lines 89-91), so sharpened suffixes never carry over to a step where they would execute.

confirmed.description: As described, with three precision corrections: (1) horizon 6 is a CLI default (plan/cem.py:184), not hardcoded, and lies within §5's H ∈ {3..8}, so it is an untested-sweep issue, not a spec deviation; (2) the ≥200-episode and 300×30 figures are §7 formal-run commitments and finding 18 frames this run as a tier-1 machinery check with the iters reduction disclosed — the defect is that the episode-count shortfall is not flagged and the verdict is generalized beyond the tested configuration, not that the deviations were concealed; (3) the 'same seed-0 episode set' claim for the tuning rounds is inferred from CLI defaults and deterministic episode sampling, not provable from artifacts (runs/tier1/ preserves only the final invocation). confirmed.title: The tier-2 trainer lacks the nonfinite-step guard the project built for exactly this machine's MPS failure mode, and 'device-numerics-sensitive, itself a mark against the shape' converts a known harness gap into architectural evidence
confirmed.location: training loop lines 304-320 (no isfinite check; contrast train/train.py:221-229); claim at terminal-jepa-status.md line 66
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 16's 'its round-1 collapse was device-numerics-sensitive, itself a mark against the shape'
confirmed.description: The status doc's operational learnings (line 119) record that MPS on this machine produces nonfinite values under memory pressure at ~2% of steps, root-caused as environmental ('an identical replay ran clean'), and that train/train.py therefore skips any step with nonfinite loss or grad norm. models/tier2.py has no such guard — it only clips grad norm, and clip_grad_norm_ does not protect against NaN/inf gradients (it scales by a nonfinite factor). editpred round 1 ran on MPS during a window with documented memory pressure (two jetsam kills recorded around the rerun attempts), destabilized late (loss 0.46->1.79 over the last 800 steps, monitor EM collapsing), and trained clean on CPU. That pattern is at least as consistent with 'the documented environmental MPS failure hit an unguarded trainer' as with 'the editpred shape is numerics-fragile' — and no control separates them: slotpred was never trained on CPU, editpred never rerun on MPS with a guard. Independent of the attribution, the missing guard is a live harness defect: the declared winner slotpred-v2 itself trained on MPS (device='auto'), and any future tier-2 or bake-off rerun on this machine can be silently corrupted the same way.
confirmed.evidence: grep of both trainers (only train/train.py has isfinite/skipped logic); runs/tier2/editpred/train_log.json (loss 0.464 @2200 -> 1.785 @3000, invalid EM 0.885 -> 0.545); runs/tier2/editpred-v2/train_log.json (CPU, loss 0.045, monotone-improving EM); terminal-jepa-status.md line 119 (MPS NaN root-caused environmental, guard added to the main trainer) and line 66 (jetsam kills preceding the CPU rerun); ckpt train_args showing slotpred-v2 on 'auto' (MPS).
affected_verdicts: Finding 16 (tier-2 winner slotpred) — the winner verdict itself stands via the editpred-v2 CPU confirmation rerun, but its supporting claim 'round-1 collapse was device-numerics-sensitive, itself a mark against the shape' is unsupported attribution and must be re-scoped

confirmed.title: build_valid_pool orders its candidate pool by salted set-hash order, making the filtered planner arms irreproducible across processes
confirmed.location: line 135 (build_valid_pool), consumed at lines 102 and 124
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 18's filtered numbers (cem-filtered 73% vs random-filtered 76%, delta -3) in runs/tier1/cem-oracle.json, and the reproducibility premise of future §7 formal Phase-2 runs
confirmed.description: build_valid_pool returns list({actions.sample_valid(state, rng) for _ in range(draws)}) — a set of string tuples whose iteration order depends on Python's per-process string-hash salt (PYTHONHASHSEED). Every subsequent valid_pool[rng.randrange(len(valid_pool))] draw therefore picks a different action across processes even with identical --seed: the index-to-action mapping is scrambled while the rng stream is unchanged. Verified empirically: two processes with the same rng seed produced entirely different pool orderings. Every other RNG path in the project is deliberately string-keyed and process-stable, and sample_valid/sample_invalid/make_satisfying all sort before choosing — this is the single hash-order leak. Effect: the recorded filtered success rates (73/76) are one nondeterministic realization; a rerun of the exact command gives different filtered numbers, and the -3 filtered delta can move or flip sign at n=100. Unfiltered arms and the scripted ceiling never touch the pool and are reproducible. No bias is introduced (the pool draw is uniform either way), so the directional verdict stands, but the §7 pre-registered protocol (paired, seeded runs at the +30 bar) inherits an irreproducible harness. Fix is one word: sorted(...) instead of list({...}).
confirmed.evidence: Read plan/cem.py:131-135; ran the same seeded pool construction in two separate python3 processes: first-5 elements differed completely; confirmed pool is consumed by index at cem.py:102 and 124; status doc 'Operational learnings' on string-keyed process-stable RNG.

confirmed.title: build_valid_pool's 300-draw approximation covers ~7% of the valid action space; the filtered arms' 73-76% plateau is quantitatively bounded by this hardcoded knob, not by search
confirmed.location: lines 131-135 (build_valid_pool, draws=300 hardcoded)
confirmed.claim_attacked: Finding 18's 'machinery validated (filtered success 73-76% vs scripted ceiling 100%)' and its failure attribution for the residual ~25%
confirmed.description: build_valid_pool (plan/cem.py:131-135) approximates the valid-action set with 300 sample_valid draws — measured on the reconstructed episode start states this dedups to a median pool of ~188 of a median 2,636 valid actions (~7% coverage; valid-set min/med/max 1031/2636/7438, reproduced exactly). Because filtered planners always execute a pool-drawn seq[0], filtered episode success is hard upper-bounded by the pool's needle-supply rate compounded over ≤12 replans, and that bound is monotone in the hardcoded, sensitivity-unchecked draws parameter. For the 89 file_exists_with_class episodes, only 2-3/40 sampled start-state pools contain any one-step satisfying action, and per-rebuild P(write-needle in pool) is 3.4-10.9%. Caveat vs the original claim: write-needle compounding alone predicts ~53% overall filtered success, not the observed 73-76 — the remainder comes from other pool-supplied channels (cp/mv of an existing right-class file, mkdir-then-write), which are also draws-limited, so the plateau is bounded and largely explained by pool supply but not point-matched by the write-needle arithmetic alone. Finding 18 already credits the filter's gains to needle-supply and CEM-vs-random is pool-symmetric, so no verdict flips (severity correctly minor); what the record omits is that the 73-76 level and the ~25-point gap to the scripted ceiling reflect the draws=300 knob rather than any property of search or cost, and gate 3's reported filtered-vs-filtered numbers run through the same code.

confirmed.title: The needle-mechanism sentence in finding 18 is inaccurate in both directions: the needle is not unique, and for many start states there is NO one-step satisfying action
